notes: Self-correction iteration: within ONE user turn aider runs a shell tool call that fails, interprets the failure, and retries with corrected input — multiple tool-call/result cycles back to back — and the whole arc stays a SINGLE contiguous working span with no flicker to ready or waiting between attempts, settling to ready only at the single turn end. WHY this is one turn for aider: io.user_input() writes the `#### ` history header exactly once per top-level prompt, BEFORE Coder.run_one; run_one's `while message:` reflection loop (max_reflections=3) drives the retry by setting reflected_message and calling send_message directly, never re-emitting a `#### ` header (base_coder.py:860/879/924-944). So the fail→retry→recover arc is one logical turn from the markdown transcript's point of view. The parser deliberately treats every `> Tokens: …` line as assistant_message (NOT turn_done) so the turn stays open across multiple model calls (parser.go:18-23,146-188); `> Running <cmd>`→tool_result(Bash) and `> Applied edit to`→tool_result(Edit) (parser.go:67-70,119-124,231-242) never change state away from working. So the topology this spec asserts — one contiguous working span spanning N tool-call/result pairs and N model calls, then ready — is exactly what the parser is built to emit. SESSION IDENTITY (differs from pi/codex; same as aider/auto-executed-tool-call): aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD, RawLineParser), so the daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session and no handoff/reconcile phase. Every event (transcript_new, presession_created, the state arc, pid_discovered, process_exited) lands on the one proc-<PID> row, so the turn arc, pid_bind and teardown all pin to session_birth via same_session_as. To avoid a transient proc-<PID> presession row stealing identity, session_birth anchors to recording start UNPINNED; turn_start (the first real working) establishes the bindable session and turn_end/teardown chain off session_birth's match. The intermediate_working phase pins the no-flicker invariant with duration_at_least_ms 3000 — long enough to span both retry cycles given typical aider latency; if the parser ever surfaces a failing tool_result as a transition to ready or waiting, this phase fires. The failing-then-recovery tool calls are observable in transcript.jsonl as aider tool_result markers (Bash); the phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the retry cycle is asserted via the single sustained working span plus the transcript pairs, not a dedicated phase — and tool visibility is COARSE (one marker line per tool, no args/result payload). Recorded with the INTERACTIVE aider driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh, a tmux REPL), NOT headless `aider --message`. WHY interactive: aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1.5s quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run can exit promptly after the reply so the daemon misses the terminal working→ready settle before process_exited, truncating the arc. Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn (which blocks on the `> Tokens:` close) plus a ≥6s trailing sleep (one full idle-flush cycle plus slack) lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle cleanly, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited. SCOPE NOTE / observes:partial (idle-flush flicker risk — this spec asserts the no-flicker IDEAL): the one documented way this target is NOT guaranteed is the 1500ms IdleFlush (aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter, parser.go:54,208-229). Between two reflection cycles the only activity is a model call and the transcript is silent for its whole duration; if any single inter-reflection model call exceeds ~1.5s of file silence, IdleFlush fires a premature turn_done (working→ready) and the next `> Tokens:` line in the SAME logical turn reopens it (ready→working) — the exact flicker intermediate_working forbids. A fast local model that finishes each call inside the window avoids this; a slow remote model can trip it. The root cause is timing, not topology — hence the coverage matrix verdict observes:partial. If a real recording trips the flicker, that is a known timing limitation to flag (do NOT widen this spec to accept the flicker — it documents the target). HOW runs differ: exact assistant wording, the shell tool's error text, per-turn token counts, and sub-second timing vary (model/OS-dependent); the working→ready edge lands with ~5s effective idle-flush latency (a timing delay, not a correctness gap). Stable structurally: one ready→working→ready arc on the one proc-<PID> session with multiple Bash tool_result markers (at least one failing) inside the single working span, a single process_exited, and a final ready state — never waiting. NOTE: recording deferred — no aider auth in this environment (no provider key; headless aider hangs ~5min on provider OAuth; the rig precheck delegates auth to the user). This expected.jsonl is the DOCUMENTED TARGET the recording will validate against once auth is available.

text: Session appears in ready within 1s of agent launch
User prompt requiring tool use → working; the failing shell call and its corrective retry both run inside this span (spec bullet 1)
State stays continuously working across all retry cycles — the failing shell tool_result must NOT flicker the session to ready or waiting before the recovery cycle starts (spec bullet 2)
After the final successful attempt the single turn completes → working → ready; state never entered waiting (spec bullet 3)
REPL torn down once after the turn → a single process_exited on the session; state immediately before teardown is ready
